nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; found in coral reef gullies at shallow depths of approximately 1-6 m, usually hovering at the entrances of holes, caves, and overhangs

diet.notes: Generally advised to offer meaty marine foods such as mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped fresh or frozen marine fish, squid, copepods, and frozen carnivore preparations. In the wild, has been documented feeding on small zooplanktonic crustaceans. Feeding at least twice daily is commonly recommended. Some specimens have been reported to accept quality pellets or flakes over time.

avoidWith: fish of similar shape and territory

summary: A small, splendidly blue cave-dwelling reef fish from the Western Pacific (Japan, Taiwan, Philippines), generally considered suitable for beginner reef aquarists. Known for its characteristic upside-down swimming under ledges.
careNotes:
Randall's Assessor Basslet (Assessor randalli) is a small, cryptic, cave-associated reef fish first described by Allen & Kuiter in 1976 and originating from the Western Pacific (Japan, Taiwan, and the Philippines). It is generally considered one of the more approachable small basslets for beginner to intermediate marine aquarists. Like its congeners, it tends to spend much of its time near caves and overhangs, often swimming upside down — a natural behavior tied to its cave-dwelling lifestyle rather than a sign of illness. Ample live rock with caves and crevices is widely regarded as essential for this species' wellbeing.

Temperament: Sources disagree on disposition. LiveAquaria and Quality Marine describe it as semi-aggressive and territorial (especially toward conspecific males and other basslets), while Liverocknreef and Bulk Reef Supply describe it as generally peaceful or timid. The consensus appears to be that it is peaceful toward most unrelated species but becomes territorial toward conspecific males and fish of similar shape occupying comparable cave space. Best kept singly or as a confirmed mated pair; multiple males in the same system are generally reported to be problematic.

Family note: LiveAquaria lists the family as Grammidae, but FishBase and major taxonomic authorities place the genus Assessor in Plesiopidae (Roundheads). Plesiopidae is used here.

Size note: FishBase records a maximum of 4.0 cm TL from wild measurements, while hobby sources cite 2 inches (~5 cm); Liverocknreef's '4 inches' figure appears to be an outlier and is inconsistent with the broader literature for the genus. The recorded 3.5-5 cm range averages the credible figures.

Temperature note: Hobby sources cluster around 72-78°F (22.2-25.6°C); Bulk Reef Supply cites 75-80°F (23.9-26.7°C) and FishBase wild data indicates approximately 27°C in the Western Pacific habitat. The recorded 23-26.5°C range averages these.

Tank size: Most hobby sources cite a 30-gallon (~114 L) minimum for a single specimen. The recorded 120 L biases slightly upward per the cautious tank-size policy and accommodates the rockwork-heavy aquascape this species needs to feel secure and display naturally.

Reproduction: Like other Assessor species, the male is reported to perform paternal mouthbrooding, incubating the egg mass for roughly two weeks. Captive-bred specimens (notably from ORA) have been produced and generally acclimate more readily than wild-caught fish.

Reef safety: Generally considered fully reef-safe — does not pose a threat to corals or sessile invertebrates. Very small ornamental shrimp could theoretically be at risk given the species' carnivorous diet, but predation is not commonly reported.

Jumping: Quality Marine notes that a tightly-fitting lid is advised, as the species has been known to jump from open aquariums when startled — a typical caution for small reef fishes.
breedingNotes: Paternal mouthbrooding has been documented for the genus Assessor; the male incubates the egg mass in his mouth for roughly two weeks. Captive breeding has been achieved (ORA captive-bred specimens are commercially available). Specific breeding-difficulty data for A. randalli is limited in the hobby literature.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Plesiopidae confirmed, correcting LiveAquaria's Grammidae listing), authority (Allen & Kuiter, 1976), maximum size (4.0 cm TL), native range (Western Pacific, 28°N-30°S, 119°E-154°E), depth range (1-6 m), habitat (coral reef gullies; nervous at hole entrances), and confirmation of paternal egg-guarding behavior.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing (2 in), tank size (30 gal), temperature (72-78°F), pH (8.1-8.4), salinity (1.020-1.025), dKH (8-12), reef-safe status, and color development. Also flagged the 'Coral Assessor' trade name and a jumping caution.

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply
additional.notes: Additional data point covering the genus Assessor: 30 gal minimum, 75-80°F (23.9-26.7°C), pH 7.7-8.5, salinity 1.020-1.026, peaceful temperament with preference for shaded areas, and reef-safe.

additional.name: Liverocknreef
additional.notes: Confirmed Western Pacific origin (Japan, Taiwan), Blue Assessor trade name, peaceful/timid temperament, and reef-safe status. Outlier size figure (4 in) was disregarded in favor of FishBase + LiveAquaria consensus.

fish.breedingNotes: Genus Assessor are paternal mouthbrooders; males incubate the egg mass for approximately two weeks. Captive-bred A. randalli specimens (notably from ORA) have been produced. Specific difficulty rating for this species is not well-established in the hobby literature.
